summary: Carbondale's affordable-housing pipeline anchors on the Town Center Property — 14 vacant downtown parcels gifted to the Town in December 2021 (≈$2.4M total value) — being developed with Artspace Projects, Inc. into a Phase 1 of 39 affordable artist-workforce units. The Town funds its housing programs through a voter-approved 6% short-term-rental excise tax, has built a ≈$1M housing fund through annual budget transfers, and participates in the regional West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition's Good Deeds buy-down program.

paragraphs.text: In December 2021 the Town of Carbondale received 14 vacant parcels at the downtown Town Center site as a donation from anonymous donors, with the gift valued at approximately $2.4 million. The 1.4-acre site sits near Thunder River Theatre in the heart of downtown and anchors the Town's current affordable / creative housing pipeline.

The Town received eight responses to its initial developer RFP, narrowed the field to five finalists (Artspace, Gorman & Company, Habitat for Humanity, RealAmerica, and Western Slope Consulting), and selected Artspace Projects, Inc. as the master developer.

Artspace is partnered with the State of Colorado's Space to Create Colorado program — a first-in-the-nation, state-led initiative focused on affordable housing for residents in the creative sector in rural areas — and is expected to manage the completed project, contracting a local property-management firm for day-to-day operations.

Phase 1 is programmed for 39 residential units plus approximately 2,000 sq ft of commercial space. The 39-unit mix: 6 studios (≈500 sf), 10 one-bedroom (≈695 sf), 19 two-bedroom (835–1,115 sf), and 4 three-bedroom (1,080–1,190 sf). Affordability targets cover 80–150% Area Median Income (AMI).

The Carbondale Board of Trustees approved the Phase 1 schematic design in early 2024 to support a 9% Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) application to the Colorado Housing and Finance Authority (CHFA). Per the Town's published timeline, the earliest construction start was 2025 with first occupancy targeted for 2026.

Carbondale voters approved a 6% excise tax on short-term-rental accommodations in November 2022, with revenues dedicated to the Town's affordable-housing fund. The tax is layered on top of the existing 2% lodging tax. Initial revenue estimates ranged from approximately $150,000 to $400,000 per year depending on the source.

As of November 2023, the Board of Trustees had built the Town's housing fund to approximately $1 million through annual budget transfers and conservative spending, with the fund explicitly available to advance the Town's housing goals including the Town Center development.

The Town adopted its first Community Housing Plan in 2023, building on prior Housing Guidelines and on the work of the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition. The Plan is the policy framework that organizes the Town Center development, the Town's deed-restricted Community Housing Program, and participation in regional initiatives.
On November 12, 2024 the Board approved Ordinance No. 10, Series of 2024, amending the Town's inclusionary residential housing requirements.

Carbondale contracts with the Garfield County Housing Authority to manage qualifications, sales, improvement approvals, and other compliance aspects of the Town's deed-restricted Community Housing Program.

sections.heading: Regional context — West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition

paragraphs.text: The West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC) is a tax-exempt nonprofit formed in 2022 to increase community-housing availability across the Roaring Fork and Middle Colorado River valleys. Carbondale is a participating municipality alongside other regional jurisdictions; the coalition's geographic scope spans Garfield, Pitkin, and Eagle counties.

WMRHC operates the 'Good Deeds' buy-down program, modeled on a similarly-named Eagle County program. The program subsidizes the purchase of free-market homes by 30% in exchange for a permanent deed restriction that caps the home's annual price appreciation at 3% based on the buyer's original purchase price. The deed restriction creates a sub-market of below-market-rate ownership homes for the regional workforce.

Carbondale appropriated funding jointly with the Town of Glenwood Springs — the two municipalities together committed approximately $300,000 to support the Good Deeds program. Carbondale-area home buy-down price points have been quoted around $1.2 million.
